7: wayyitser YHWH Elohim et-haadam afar min-haadam wayyipach beapav nishmat hayyim wayyehi haadam lenefesh hayyah: 'YHWH God formed the man of dust from the ground and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life, and the man became a living creature (nefesh hayyah).' Two observations: (1) wayyitser (formed) uses the imagery of the potter (yatsar) — human embodiment is the result of divine craftsmanship, not accident; (2) nishmat hayyim (breath of life) — the identical phrase is used for all animals in 7:22; what distinguishes humanity is not the breath but the preceding formation in the divine image (1:26-27) and the naming task (2:19-20). Nefesh hayyah (living being/soul) is not a Platonic immortal soul but the whole animated creature — biblical anthropology is holistic: the person is the ensouled body, not a soul housed in a body.
24: al-ken yaazov ish et-aviv veimmo vedavak beishto vehayyu levasar echad (ʿal-kēn yaʿăzŏv ʾîš ʾet-ʾābîw wĕʾimmô wĕdābaq bĕʾištô wĕhāyû lĕbāśār ʾeḥād): 'Therefore a man shall leave his father and his mother and hold fast to his wife, and they shall become one flesh.' The narrative aside (al-ken, therefore/for this reason) signals a foundational principle being derived from the narrative. Davak (hold fast/cleave) is the same word used for Israel's covenant-commitment to YHWH (Deut 10:20; Josh 23:8) — marital union is described in covenant-fidelity language. Basar echad (one flesh): not merely physical union but a comprehensive sharing of life, identity, and destiny — which is why Paul calls it a 'profound mystery' (mysterion mega) of the Christ-church relationship.
1:
וַיְכֻלּוּ (wayĕkullû) — Pual imperfect of kālāh (to be complete, finished, brought to an end). The passive voice: the heavens and earth were completed — they brought themselves to completion under God's creative word. The same root kālāh will describe the completion of the tabernacle (Exod 40:33) and Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 6:9; 7:51) — a structural echo suggesting that creation itself is a cosmic sanctuary.

צְבָאָם (ṣĕbāʾām) — "their host/array." Ṣābāʾ (army, host) is used of the stars (Deut 4:19; Isa 45:12) and of the angels (1 Kgs 22:19). The term frames creation as an ordered host under the supreme commander — God as the LORD of Hosts (YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt) is already implicit in the creational completion.
2:
וַיִּשְׁבֹּת (wayyišbōt) — "and he rested/ceased." The root is šābat, from which "Sabbath" (šabbāt) derives. The word means to stop, cease — not to recover from fatigue (Exod 31:17 explicitly says God did not become weary). God's rest is the completion-rest of a creator whose work is finished, not the rest of a laborer. The Sabbath commandment (Exod 20:11; Deut 5:14-15) points back to this verse as its foundation — Israel rests because God rested.

מְלַאכְתּוֹ (mĕlaʾktô) — "his work." The same word is used of the skilled craftwork of the tabernacle artisans (Exod 35-36). The creation of the cosmos and the construction of the sanctuary are described in parallel vocabulary — the priestly authors of ch 1-2 may have intentionally structured the comparison.
3:
וַיְקַדֵּשׁ אֹתוֹ (wayĕqaddēš ʾōtô) — "and he sanctified/made it holy." Qādaš (Piel) means to set apart as holy, consecrate. The seventh day is the first holy thing in Genesis — before the tabernacle, before Sinai, God establishes holiness in time. The Sabbath is a sanctuary in time rather than space, and its sanctification precedes all human worship structures.

The seventh day notably lacks the evening-morning closing formula used for days 1-6. The rabbis observed this absence and interpreted the seventh day as having no end — open-ended, still continuing. Hebrews 4:9-10 builds on this: there remains a Sabbath-rest for the people of God.
4:
אֵלֶּה תוֹלְדוֹת (ʾēlleh tôlĕḏôt) — "these are the generations/account of." The formula tôlĕḏôt (from yālad, to bear/beget) recurs ten times in Genesis as a structural marker (2:4; 5:1; 6:9; 10:1; 11:10; 11:27; 25:12; 25:19; 36:1; 37:2). It introduces either a genealogy or a narrative account of what something or someone generated/produced. Here it looks backward: the account of how heavens and earth came to be.

יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים (YHWH ʾĕlōhîm) — the divine name YHWH (the LORD, the personal covenantal name) first appears at 2:4. From here through ch 3, the compound "the LORD God" (YHWH ʾĕlōhîm) is used consistently. The shift from ʾĕlōhîm alone (ch 1) to the compound name (ch 2-3) has long been noted — it introduces the personal, relational, covenant-making God who enters into direct relationship with humanity.
5: The verse uses Hebrew circumstantial construction to set the scene before the narrative: "when no shrub... had yet sprung up — for the LORD God had not sent rain... and no man was there to work the ground." This pluperfect-style background clause (the Qal perfect with waw is often pluperfect in context) establishes that ch 2:4ff. is not a sequential continuation of ch 1's day 6 but a close-up on the forming of man before vegetation was established. The agricultural focus (ʿāḇad hāʾăḏāmāh, work the ground) is deliberate: the ground needs a worker, and God provides one.
6: אֵד (ʾēḏ) — "mist" or "spring/stream." The term is rare (only here and Job 36:27); its precise meaning is contested. Cognate Akkadian edu means underground water/flood. Most modern scholars favor "springs" or "underground water" rising to water the surface — setting the agricultural scene before rain. The MKT's "streams" reflects this.
8: גַּן-בְּעֵדֶן מִקֶּדֶם (gan-bĕʿēḏen miqqeḏem) — "a garden in Eden, in the east." ʿĒḏen may derive from the Sumerian/Akkadian edinu (steppe, plain) or from the Hebrew ʿēḏen (pleasure, luxury — Ps 36:8). The geographic "east" places Eden in the mythological/primordial east — the direction of sunrise and of origins. The garden is a subset within Eden: God planted a garden in Eden, not identified with all of Eden.
9:
עֵץ הַחַיִּים (ʿēṣ haḥayyîm) — "the tree of life." Life is a gift mediated through this tree; access to it sustains ongoing life (3:22 confirms that eating from it would grant immortality). The tree recurs in Proverbs (3:18; 11:30; 13:12; 15:4) as a wisdom metaphor, and in Revelation (2:7; 22:2, 14, 19) as the restored eschatological gift. The loss of access to the tree of life (3:24) is the fundamental consequence of the Fall; restoration to it is the fundamental promise of the new creation.

עֵץ הַדַּעַת טוֹב וָרָע (ʿēṣ haddaʿat ṭôb wārāʿ) — "the tree of the knowledge of good and evil." Daʿat is experiential knowledge — not mere information but the knowing that comes from engagement. Ṭôb wārāʿ (good and evil) as a merism may mean comprehensive moral knowledge, or the ability to determine for oneself what is good and evil (the usurpation of divine prerogative). The serpent's promise (3:5) uses exactly this phrase.
10: The river description establishes the garden as the source of the world's water supply — creation's life flows outward from Eden. The fourfold division of rivers from a single source is geographic-theological: Eden is the fountainhead. Ezekiel 47 and Revelation 22 both invoke the same image of a river flowing from God's presence to sustain creation.
11: הַפִּישׁוֹן — the Pishon is unidentified with certainty; some locate it in Arabia (associated with gold and bdellium). The detail of Havilah's gold (zāhāb) is the only other geographic anchor. The geographic specificity grounds the narrative in real geography without allowing exact modern identification — intentional or simply reflecting ancient knowledge the author assumed.
12: בְּדֹלַח (bĕḏōlaḥ) — "bdellium," an aromatic resin (cf. Num 11:7 where manna is compared to it). שֹׁהַם (šōham) — "onyx" or "carnelian" (the exact stone is debated); it appears on the high priest's ephod (Exod 28:9, 20). The precious materials of the land around Eden foreshadow the materials used in the tabernacle and temple — Eden as the original sanctuary, the temple as Eden-recreation.
13: The Gihon is the name of the spring of Jerusalem (1 Kgs 1:33, 38, 45), associated with the city of David. Whether the Genesis Gihon is the same location or a symbolic naming, the connection links Eden's water-source with Jerusalem's central spring — the city of God as the restored Eden-center of creation.
14: חִדֶּקֶל (ḥiddeqel) — the Tigris, consistently identified (Dan 10:4; Assyrian Idiqlat). פְּרָת (pĕrāt) — the Euphrates. Two of the four rivers are the great rivers of Mesopotamia — the historical-geographic frame of ANE civilization. The Eden narrative is set in recoverable geography even if the garden itself is inaccessible (3:24). The two known rivers anchor the narrative in history.
15: לְעָבְדָהּ וּלְשָׁמְרָהּ (lĕʿāḇdāh ûlĕšāmĕrāh) — "to work it and to keep/guard it." ʿĀḇaḏ is the word for service, work, and also worship (ʿăbōḏāh = service/liturgy); šāmar is to guard, keep, watch (used of keeping commandments and keeping the sanctuary). Both terms are used together for the Levitical service of the tabernacle (Num 3:7-8; 8:26; 18:5-6). The man is placed in the garden as a priest-king: his mandate is both cultivation and guardianship, both priestly service and royal oversight.
16: כֹּל עֵץ הַגָּן אָכֹל תֹּאכֵל (kōl ʿēṣ haggan ʾākōl tōʾkēl) — the permission is stated with the emphatic infinitive absolute + finite verb (ʾākōl tōʾkēl) — "you may freely eat." The scope of freedom is maximized before the limit is stated. The command in v. 17 comes as restriction within a broadly generous gift, not as a primary prohibition.
17: מוֹת תָּמוּת (môt tāmût) — infinitive absolute + finite verb: "you will certainly die" or "dying you will die." The emphatic construction underscores the certainty and totality of the consequence. The serpent's counter (lōʾ mōt tĕmutûn, 3:4) uses the same construction negated — the debate is over the certainty of this very phrase. The death threatened may encompass separation from God, expulsion from the garden, spiritual death, and eventual physical death — all of which follow in ch 3. That the man does not physically die immediately (he lives to 930 years, 5:5) has suggested to many interpreters that the death is first relational/spiritual, with physical death as the ultimate expression.
18:
לֹא-טוֹב (lōʾ-ṭôb) — "it is not good." The first lōʾ-ṭôb in the creation narrative — the sevenfold good of ch 1 is here interrupted for the first time. The deficit is not in creation itself but in the incompleteness of the human situation: alone, without a corresponding partner.

עֵזֶר כְּנֶגְדּוֹ (ʿēzer kĕneḡdô) — "a helper corresponding to him" or "a helper as his counterpart." ʿĒzer (helper) is used of God himself as helper to Israel (Ps 121:2; 124:8; Exod 18:4) — the word carries no connotation of inferiority; it describes the role of the one whose strength supplies what is lacking in the other. Kĕneḡdô means "as before him," "face-to-face," "his match" — the preposition negeḏ indicates correspondence and facing, not subordination. The woman is the man's equal counterpart who faces him, not an assistant beneath him.
19: The Qal perfect yāṣar ("had formed") here is likely a pluperfect — the animals had already been formed; now they are brought to the man. The narrative interest is not the sequence of creation but the human act of naming. God brings the animals to the man to see "what he would call them" (mah-yiqrāʾ-lô) — God observes with interest the man's exercise of dominion through naming.
20: The man names all the animals — exercising the image-bearing dominion of 1:26-28 in the most concrete form. וּלְאָדָם לֹא מָצָא (ûlĕʾāḏām lōʾ māṣāʾ) — "but for the man no helper corresponding to him was found." The naming of animals establishes by negative evidence the uniqueness of humanity's need: no animal corresponds to the man. This sets up the creation of woman not as an afterthought but as the necessary completion that the animal parade has demonstrated to be missing.
21:
תַּרְדֵּמָה (tarḏēmāh) — "deep sleep." The same word is used of Abraham's deep sleep during the covenant ceremony (15:12) and of Saul's sleep in the camp (1 Sam 26:12). It is a special, often divinely-induced sleep that accompanies supernatural divine action. The man is passive and unconscious while God acts — the woman's creation is entirely God's work.

אַחַת מִצַּלְעֹתָיו (ʾaḥat miṣṣalʿōtāyw) — "one of his ribs/sides." Ṣēlāʿ is used elsewhere for the side of the ark (Exod 25:12; 37:3) and the side chamber of the temple (1 Kgs 6:34; Ezek 41:5-11). It can mean "rib" (the narrow sense) or "side" (the broader construction sense). Some interpreters read "side" here — God takes one side of the man to form the woman, resulting in two beings from one original. The MKT's "rib" follows the standard English tradition.
22: וַיִּבֶן יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים אֶת-הַצֵּלָע (wayyiḇen YHWH ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-haṣṣēlāʿ) — "and the LORD God built/fashioned the rib." The verb bānāh (build) is used here rather than yāṣar (form/shape) or bārāʾ (create). Bānāh is the builder's verb — used of building houses, cities, walls, and the temple. Woman is built/constructed, not merely shaped. The result is then brought (wayyĕbīʾehā) to the man — God as the one who presents the bride, a motif with a long trajectory.
23:
זֹאת הַפַּעַם (zōʾt happaʿam) — "this at last" or "this one, now!" The phrase is exclamatory and immediate — Adam's first recorded speech is a recognition and celebration. עֶצֶם מֵעֲצָמַי וּבָשָׂר מִבְּשָׂרִי — "bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh" — a covenant kinship formula (used in 2 Sam 5:1; 19:12-13 for tribal solidarity). The man recognizes the woman as belonging to him in the deepest way — not possessively but kinship-relationally.

לְזֹאת יִקָּרֵא אִשָּׁה כִּי מֵאִישׁ לֻקֳחָה זֹּאת — the ʾîš/ʾiššāh wordplay is explicit: "she shall be called ʾiššāh (woman) because from ʾîš (man) she was taken." The Hebrew pun is the man's own discovery — the naming arises from recognition of origin and correspondence. English "woman/man" or "she-man/man" approximates the wordplay; no translation fully preserves it.
25:
עֲרוּמִּים (ʿărûmmîm) — "naked." The word sets up the deliberate wordplay with the serpent's ʿārûm (crafty/shrewd, 3:1) — both words share the same consonantal root in Hebrew. The man and woman are ʿārûmmîm (naked, without concealment) while the serpent is ʿārûm (crafty, without revealing his intent). The innocence of nakedness without shame is the condition the serpent's craftiness will undo.

וְלֹא יִתְבֹּשָׁשׁוּ (wĕlōʾ yitbōšāšû) — "and they were not ashamed." The verb bûš (ashamed, put to shame) denotes the social experience of being exposed and found deficient. Their nakedness produced no shame because there was no covering needed — no moral deficit, no rupture in relationship to God or each other. The contrast with 3:7-10 (shame, hiding, covering) makes the transition from innocence to guilt viscerally apparent.
